title: Akamai Guardicore Segmentation Policy Violations
dataSources: Akamai Guardicore REST API or syslog via the Add-on
spl:
index=microseg sourcetype="guardicore:network" earliest=-24h
| where match(lower(action),"(?i)block|deny|drop|reject")
| stats count by src_asset, dst_asset, dst_port, policy_name
| sort -count
description: Akamai Guardicore enforces microsegmentation at the workload level across on-prem, cloud, and hybrid environments. Policy violation events (blocked lateral movement) validate that default-deny segmentation is enforced and detect attempted east-west attacks.
value: Zero Trust teams track Akamai Guardicore segmentation policy violations over time, detecting lateral movement attempts (one source scanning many targets), distinguishing legitimate application changes from attacker behavior, and identifying new workloads deployed without segmentation policies.
implementation: Install the Akamai Guardicore Add-on. Map `src_asset` and `dst_asset` to application labels from the Guardicore Reveal map. Alert on blocked flows to critical assets (databases, domain controllers). Report on deny-to-allow ratio per segmentation ring.
visualization: Table (blocked flows), Heatmap (source × destination), Bar chart (blocks by policy), Sankey (traffic flow map).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Change.All_Changes
  by Change.user, Change.object, Change.action span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Microseg blocks from new app deployments, missing exception rules, or planned segmentation rollouts.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Akamai Guardicore Centra logs in `index=microseg` with `sourcetype=guardicore:syslog`. Key fields: `src_ip`, `dst_ip`, `src_label`, `dst_label`, `policy_name`, `action` (allow/block), `rule_type` (specific/default), `dstPort`, `proto`.
• Guardicore Segmentation creates micro-perimeters around workloads. Policy violations = traffic blocked by segmentation rules. This UC is specific to Guardicore in the Zero Trust context (separate from UC-17.6.x which covers Guardicore in the general microsegmentation category).

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify Guardicore events:
```spl
index=microseg sourcetype="guardicore:syslog" action=block earliest=-24h
| stats count by src_label, dst_label, dstPort
| sort - count
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Policy violation trends:
```spl
index=microseg sourcetype="guardicore:syslog" action=block earliest=-7d
| timechart span=1h count by policy_name limit=10
```

Understanding this SPL: Trends segmentation policy violations over time. A spike in violations on one policy means either: a legitimate application changed its communication pattern (needs policy update), an attacker is attempting lateral movement (blocked by policy), or a new workload was deployed without a corresponding policy.

Lateral movement indicators:
```spl
index=microseg sourcetype="guardicore:syslog" action=block earliest=-24h
| stats count dc(dst_ip) as targets dc(dstPort) as ports by src_ip, src_label
| where targets > 10 OR ports > 10
| sort - targets
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In Guardicore Centra: Reveal tab > filter by blocked traffic.
(b) Attempt traffic between restricted segments. Verify block.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Violation trend. Row 2 — Lateral movement suspects. Row 3 — New violations.
Alert: When lateral movement pattern detected (one source, many targets).

Runbook:
1. **Lateral movement pattern** — Possible breach. Isolate source immediately.
2. **Policy violation after deployment** — New app needs policy. Create after verification.
3. **Spike after policy change** — New policy blocking legitimate traffic. Review.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No block events** — Guardicore may be in Reveal (monitor) mode. Switch to Enforcement.
• **`policy_name` always 'Default'** — No specific policies created. Traffic falling to default deny.
grandmaExplanation: We set up invisible walls between groups of servers so even if an attacker gets into one, they cannot move to others. We watch for traffic hitting these walls — it could be a legitimate application that needs an exception, or it could be an attacker trying to spread through our network.
hardware: Akamai Guardicore Centra (management), Guardicore Agents (workload), Guardicore Aggregators
